questions.questionText: Salbutamol (albuterol) is a short-acting β2 agonist (SABA) used for:
questions.optionB: Acute relief of bronchospasm (rescue inhaler — relaxes bronchial smooth muscle via β2 receptor activation)
questions.explanation: Salbutamol: selective β2 agonist → bronchial smooth muscle relaxation (via cAMP) → bronchodilation. Rapid onset (5-15 min), short duration (4-6 hours). Rescue inhaler for acute asthma attacks, exercise-induced bronchospasm, acute exacerbation of COPD. NOT for maintenance (overuse indicates poor control). Side effects: tremor, tachycardia, hypokalemia. Long-acting β2 agonists (LABA): salmeterol, formoterol — controller drugs (always with ICS, never alone in asthma). Reference: KD Tripathi — β2 Agonists.

questions.questionText: Inhaled corticosteroids (e.g., beclomethasone, budesonide) are the cornerstone of asthma management because they:
questions.optionB: Reduce airway inflammation (suppress cytokines, eosinophils, mast cells) — most effective controller medication
questions.explanation: ICS: most potent anti-inflammatory for asthma. Mechanism: suppress multiple inflammatory genes (cytokines, chemokines, adhesion molecules, COX-2, iNOS) → reduce eosinophils, mast cells, T cells in airways. Reduce bronchial hyperreactivity, mucus secretion, edema. Mainstay of persistent asthma management. Local side effects: oral candidiasis (thrush), dysphonia (hoarseness) → prevent by rinsing mouth after use, using spacer. Systemic effects minimal with inhaled route. Reference: KD Tripathi — Inhaled Corticosteroids.

questions.questionText: Theophylline is a methylxanthine bronchodilator that acts by:
questions.optionB: Phosphodiesterase (PDE) inhibition → increased cAMP/cGMP → bronchodilation, and adenosine receptor antagonism
questions.explanation: Theophylline: methylxanthine — inhibits PDE (increases intracellular cAMP → smooth muscle relaxation) + adenosine receptor antagonism. Bronchodilator + mild anti-inflammatory + respiratory stimulant. Narrow therapeutic index (10-20 µg/mL) → requires TDM. Toxicity: seizures, cardiac arrhythmias, nausea/vomiting. Metabolism: CYP1A2 (induced by smoking, inhibited by cimetidine/erythromycin/ciprofloxacin). Uses: moderate-severe asthma (add-on), COPD, neonatal apnea (caffeine preferred). Reference: KD Tripathi — Theophylline.

questions.questionText: Ipratropium bromide is an anticholinergic used for:
questions.optionB: COPD (first-line bronchodilator) and acute asthma (added to SABA) — blocks M3 muscarinic receptors in airway smooth muscle
questions.explanation: Ipratropium: short-acting muscarinic antagonist (SAMA) — blocks M3 receptors on bronchial smooth muscle → bronchodilation (primarily in larger airways). More effective in COPD than asthma (vagal tone is predominant mechanism in COPD). Uses: COPD (with SABA for acute exacerbation), severe acute asthma (nebulized with salbutamol). Minimal systemic absorption (quaternary ammonium). Side effects: dry mouth, urinary retention (minimal systemic). Tiotropium: LAMA — once daily for COPD maintenance. Reference: KD Tripathi — Ipratropium.

questions.questionText: Omalizumab is a monoclonal antibody used in severe allergic asthma that acts by:
questions.optionB: Binding free IgE → preventing IgE from binding to mast cells and basophils → reducing allergic inflammation
questions.explanation: Omalizumab: humanized anti-IgE monoclonal antibody → binds free circulating IgE at Cε3 domain → prevents IgE from binding to FcεRI receptors on mast cells/basophils → reduces mast cell activation and mediator release. Used for moderate-severe allergic asthma unresponsive to ICS+LABA (add-on step 5). Subcutaneous injection every 2-4 weeks. Also approved for chronic urticaria. Reduces exacerbations, allows ICS dose reduction. Other biologics: mepolizumab/benralizumab (anti-IL5 — eosinophilic asthma), dupilumab (anti-IL4Rα). Reference: KD Tripathi — Omalizumab.
